title: Functions
subtitle: Build your own little machines that do a job whenever you call them.
goal: Learn to define functions with def, give them inputs (parameters), call them, and use return to send a value back — and understand how return is different from print.

blocks.heading: What is a function? 🤖
blocks.html:
A function is like a little machine or a recipe. You give it a name, teach it a job once, and then you can use it again and again just by calling its name.

Think of a microwave: you press the button (call it), it does its job (heats food), and out comes the result. You don't rebuild the microwave every time you want popcorn! 🍿

blocks.heading: Defining a function with def
blocks.html:
To make a function we use the word def (short for define), then a name, then () and a colon :. The lines that belong to the function are indented underneath.

Defining a function doesn't run it yet — it just teaches Python the recipe. To actually use it, you call it by writing its name with ().

blocks.caption: Define a function, then call it
blocks.code:
def say_hello():
    print("Hello there! 👋")

say_hello()
say_hello()
blocks.explain: def say_hello(): teaches Python the recipe. The indented line is the job. say_hello() actually runs it — and we call it twice, so we get two greetings!

blocks.html: Define first, call later. Writing def only creates the machine. Nothing happens on screen until you call it with name().

blocks.heading: Inputs: parameters and arguments 🎁
blocks.html:
Most machines need something to work on. The names inside the parentheses are parameters — little boxes that wait to be filled. The real values you hand over when you call the function are the arguments.

In greet("Sam"), the parameter is name and the argument is "Sam".

blocks.caption: A function with one input
blocks.code:
def greet(name):
    print("Hi " + name + "! Nice to see you. 😊")

greet("Sam")
greet("Maya")
blocks.explain: name is the parameter. When we call greet("Sam"), the value "Sam" gets put into name. Same machine, different inputs, different greetings!

blocks.question: Which word do we use to create a function?
blocks.explain: We use def (short for define) to create a function, like def say_hello():.

blocks.heading: return: sending a value back 📦
blocks.html:
Here's the big idea of the day. return sends a value back out of the function so the rest of your program can use it — store it, do math with it, print it later.

print just shows something on the screen and then it's gone. return hands you a real value you can keep. Big difference!

blocks.caption: return gives you a value to keep
blocks.code:
def add(a, b):
    return a + b

result = add(3, 5)
print("3 + 5 =", result)
print("Double that is", add(3, 5) * 2)
blocks.explain: add() takes two inputs and RETURNS their sum. Because it returns a value, we can save it in result AND even do more math with it (× 2). print could never do that — it only shows things.

blocks.html: return is not print! A function that only does print() shows text but hands back nothing you can use. If you want to keep or reuse the answer, you need return.

blocks.caption: A docstring describes what the function does
blocks.code:
def area_of_circle(r):
    """Return the area of a circle with radius r."""
    return 3.14159 * r * r

print("Area for radius 2:", area_of_circle(2))
print("Area for radius 5:", area_of_circle(5))
blocks.explain: The triple-quoted line right under def is a docstring — a friendly note that explains what the function does. Python ignores it when running, but it helps humans understand your machine.

blocks.heading: Why functions? Less repeating! ♻️
blocks.html:
Imagine writing the same 5 lines 10 times. Yuck! With a function you write the recipe once and call it whenever you need it. Coders call this DRY — Don't Repeat Yourself.

Functions make your code shorter, tidier, and way easier to fix — change the recipe in one place and every call gets the update. ✨

blocks.caption: A function that returns True or False
blocks.code:
def is_even(n):
    """Return True if n is an even number."""
    return n % 2 == 0

print("Is 4 even?", is_even(4))
print("Is 7 even?", is_even(7))
blocks.explain: is_even returns True or False. The % gives the remainder; if dividing by 2 leaves 0, the number is even. We reuse the same check for any number we want!

blocks.question: What is the difference between return and print?
options: There is no difference, they are the same
print shows a value on screen; return hands a value back so you can keep and reuse it
print can only be used inside functions
blocks.explain: print just displays something. return sends a real value back out of the function so you can store it, do math with it, or print it later.

blocks.question: In greet("Maya"), what is "Maya" called?
options: An argument (the value you pass in)
blocks.explain: The value you actually pass in when calling is the argument. The name waiting inside the def (like name) is the parameter.

blocks.title: Make a greet function
blocks.instructions: Finish the function so it greets the name it is given, then call it with your own name. Press Check when the output greets someone.
blocks.passMsg: Your machine works — one recipe, any name you like!
blocks.failMsg: Keep the def greet(name): line and add a call like greet("Sam") at the bottom.

blocks.title: Add with return
blocks.instructions: Write a function called add that takes two numbers and RETURNS their sum (not print!). Then print the result of adding 10 and 4.
blocks.starter:
def add(a, b):
    # use return to send back the sum
    return ___

print(add(10, 4))
blocks.passMsg: Nice — you returned a real value and printed it. That's the power of return!
blocks.failMsg: Make sure you use return a + b inside the function, then print(add(10, 4)).

challenge.title: Build a square machine
challenge.instructions: Write a function called square that takes one number and RETURNS that number multiplied by itself. Give it a docstring. Then print the square of 6 and the square of 9 so we can see it works.
challenge.starter:
def square(n):
    """Return n multiplied by itself."""
    # return the answer here
    return ___

print("6 squared is", square(6))
print("9 squared is", square(9))
challenge.solution:
def square(n):
    """Return n multiplied by itself."""
    return n * n

print("6 squared is", square(6))
print("9 squared is", square(9))
challenge.hint: Squaring means multiplying a number by itself: n * n. Use return so the answer comes back out.
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 You built a real number machine! Day 13 done — functions are now in your toolbox.
